coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic degradation study of methylene blue using carbon quantum dots synthesized from coconut husk
coredata.dc:creator: Edakkaparamban, Shuaib
Kitamura, Masaki
Ide, Yuki
Umemura, Kazuo
Ishizawa, Asuka
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a facile and one-step hydrothermal method has been followed for the synthesis of undoped carbon quantum dots (UCQDs) and N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) from naturally available coconut husk. Coconut husk is a biowaste and abundant in nature, and hence its reusability is a topic of interest. We have investigated the optical properties and photocatalytic behaviour towards methylene blue dye (MB) degradation of CQDs derived from coconut husk under white light irradiation. Also, the photocatalytic behaviour in the different pH of MB dye is studied. Our study revealed that NCQDs perform as a better photocatalyst that could degrade 94% of MB dye within 150 min and show uniform performance in the pH range from 3 to 10.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Methylene blue
Carbon quantum dot
Coconut husk

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)FTIR and (b) UV–visible spectra of UCQDs and NCQDs. AFM image and histogram of (c) UCQDs and (d) NCQDs.
UV–visible spectrum of photocatalytic degradation of MB dye using (a) UCQDs and (b) NCQDs. (c) degradation efficiency and (d) degradation rate for MB by UCQDs and NCQDs.
A plot of degradation efficiency versus time for MB dye under three different pH conditions by (a) UCQDs and (b) NCQDs.
(a) A plot of degradation rate (ln (C0/C) vs time) of MB dye by UCQDs, and (b-d) NCQDs with the pH of 3, 7 and 10.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic degradation study of methylene blue using carbon quantum dots synthesized from coconut husk

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Tokyo University of Science, 1-3 Kagurazaka, Shinjuku, Tokyo 162-8601, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Tokyo University of Science, 1-3 Kagurazaka, Shinjuku, Tokyo 162-8601, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a facile and one-step hydrothermal method has been followed for the synthesis of undoped carbon quantum dots (UCQDs) and N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) from naturally available coconut husk. Coconut husk is a biowaste and abundant in nature, and hence its reusability is a topic of interest. We have investigated the optical properties and photocatalytic behaviour towards methylene blue dye (MB) degradation of CQDs derived from coconut husk under white light irradiation. Also, the photocatalytic behaviour in the different pH of MB dye is studied. Our study revealed that NCQDs perform as a better photocatalyst that could degrade 94% of MB dye within 150 min and show uniform performance in the pH range from 3 to 10.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQD) are a new class of materials that belongs to the allotrope of carbon having a size of less than 10 nm with unique optical properties. Nowadays, researchers are attracted to CQDs due to their outstanding performance in photocatalysis, bioimaging, optoelectronic device fabrication, and nanomedicine [1]. The functional groups attached to the core of CQDs such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups bring water solubility and easy accessibility to attach with other functional species [2]. Synthesis of CQDs from various natural sources like carrot [3], turmeric [4], seaweed [5], etc., becoming a more trending topic due to its low cost, green procedure and improved optical properties. The optical properties of the CQDs depend on the selected biomass source and synthetic route. Coconut husk is used in this work as starting material for the synthesis of CQDs since it is a bio-waste and abundant in South Asian countries. Also, the photocatalytic behaviour of coconut husk-derived CQDs hasn’t been explored.

In this study, we have investigated the photocatalytic degradation of the pollutant, methylene blue which is a phenothiazine derivative mainly used for dying textiles, carcinogenic and highly toxic. Advanced oxidation techniques are required to eliminate them from the wastewater so that the organic contaminants can be degraded into harmless products. The pH of the dye plays an important role that affects the surface charges of the photocatalyst and electron-hole separation [6]. So the photocatalytic performance in the different pH of the dye is also included in this work.

Initially, 1 g of coconut husk (Ora Farm, Gunma, Japan) was mixed with 50 ml of distilled water and kept in a 100 ml Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in a furnace at a temperature of 200 °C for 6 hr duration. Then the setup was allowed to cool down and undergoes a centrifugation stage at 15000 rpm for 15 min to eliminate larger particles and unreacted husks. The supernatant obtained is labelled UCQDs. The same strategy was followed for the preparation of NCQDs where 200 mg of ammonium bicarbonate was added and dissolved in distilled water before treatment in the furnace. A brown-coloured liquid was obtained after the centrifugation process for NCQDs.
The characterization techniques and the photocatalytic experimental procedure are given in the supplementary information.

XRD reveals that the CQDs are partially crystalline and the peaks at 29° and 41° correspond to (002) and (100) crystal planes of graphitic carbon (Fig. S1). The significant surface functional groups were studied by FTIR spectroscopy. Fig. 1 (a) shows the FTIR spectra of UCQDs and NCQDs, where the prominent absorption peaks are marked. The CQDs exhibit a broad absorption band centred at 3300 cm−1 assigned to O-H stretching vibration. The absorption bands observed at 2940, 1586, 1400, and 1034 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of, C-H, C=C, COO¯ and, C-O-C group respectively [7,8]. NCQDs show an additional band at 1334 cm−1, which suggest the stretching mode of the N-(C)3 [9].

The particle size was analysed by atomic force microscopy with a scanning resolution of 2 × 2 μm (Fig. 1(c & d)). CQDs appear as irregularly-shaped quasi-spherical particles. The average particle size obtained from the height profile is calculated as 8.85 nm for UCQDs and 6.11 nm for NCQDs. There is no significant difference in the size of the particles after adding ammonium bicarbonate as a dopant.

Fig. 1(b) shows UV–visible spectra of UCQDs and NCQDs. A wide absorption band from 200 to 350 nm with its tail extended to the visible region is obtained. The UCQDs show a weak band at 225 nm, which is attributed to the n-π* transition of C=N bonds. The strong band observed at 278 nm represents the π-π* transition of the conjugated C=C bonds, which confirms the existence of an aromatic π-system in the carbon dot’s core [10]. The absorption peak of NCQDs at 270 nm indicates the transition from n-π* of C=O bonds. The absorption peak of NCQDs is slightly red-shifted due to N-doping, and the peak at 325 nm corresponds to the transition from n-π* of amide/ amine functions [11]. So, the FTIR along with Uv–Visible spectroscopy confirms that nitrogen atoms are doped onto the CQDs.

The photocatalytic activity of the synthesised CQDs was evaluated by monitoring the MB dye de-colouration under white light. MB dye shows a maximum absorption peak centred at 664 nm. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of MB dye with CQDs is shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b). The absorption peak at 664 nm is found to be gradually decreasing for UCQDS, whereas a rapid decline in the absorption is observed for NCQDs. Fig. 2(c) shows a plot of degradation efficiency versus time for both CQDs. A degradation efficiency of 76.5% is obtained after 180 min of light exposure for UCQDS, whereas it is 87% at 90 min and attained maximum efficiency of 94% after 150 min, and thereafter the de-colouration saturates for NCQDs.

The rate of degradation is analysed by fitting with a pseudo-first-order Langmuir-Hinshelwood degradation rate model expressed by the following equation,
(1)
l n
C 0
/
C t
= K t
where K is the pseudo-first-order rate constant (min−1). A linear fit to the above equation is shown in Fig. 2(d), where the slope of the linear fit of ln (C0/C) vs time plot gives the rate constant. A rate constant of 0.0081/min for UCQDs and a higher value of 0.0175/min for NCQDs were obtained. So it is concluded that by considering photocatalytic performance and degradation rate, NCQDs showed better photocatalytic activity towards the degradation of MB dye over UCQDs. The degradation process involves the adsorption of MB on the surface of CQDs. The electrons in the valance band of CQDs are excited to the conduction band under light energy and thereby produce electron-hole pairs. The photo-induced electrons react with molecular oxygen to form superoxide radicals (O2*) and water molecules react with holes to form OH* radicals. These highly reactive free radicals and holes will contribute to the decolouration of MB dye. The incorporation of nitrogen atoms in the CQDs can improve surface functionalisation, effectively induce charge localisation, prevent electron-hole recombination and hence further enhances the degradation rate.

The effect of the pH of the MB pollutant solution on photocatalytic activity was investigated. The pH of the pollutant solution is adjusted to 3, 7 and 10 by adding a few drops of diluted HCl or NaOH. The degradation efficiency is plotted in Fig. 3 for (a) UCQDs and (b) NCQDs. It is observed that the degradation efficiency is different for different pH levels and it is very low at 18.6% in acidic medium (pH 3) and it is improved to 86% in the basic medium for UNCQDs. That could be due to the increase in the hydroxyl surface groups and hence the negativity of UCQDs which acts as a surface trap causing more separation efficiency of photo-generated charge carriers. Very interestingly, this effect is very minimal for NCQDs. NCQDs show uniform behaviour for all pH levels and their degradation efficiency saturates at 93% within 150 min. The degradation rate was investigated for three pH levels and is depicted in Fig. 4 (a) for UCQDs and (b-d) for NCQDs. It is found that the degradation rate is nearly 0.002/min for NCQDs in all pH levels and it is varying from 0.0014 to 0.0131/min in the pH range from 3 to 10 for UCQDs. It can be concluded that, since the degradation rate is nearly same for the three pH levels, NCQDs are the better photocatalyst to degrade MB dye in a wide range of pH environments.

CQDs were successfully synthesised by one step hydrothermal method from naturally available coconut husk. The synthesized CQDs are effective as photocatalysts for the degradation of MB dye under white light. The UCQDs showed a degradation efficiency of 76.5% in 180 min and NCQDs exhibit 94% in 150 min. The pH of the MB dye significantly affects the photocatalytic performance of UCQDs, but NCQDs showed nearly uniform performance and hence can be used as a better photocatalyst in a wide range of pH media.

Shuaib Edakkaparamban: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Masaki Kitamura: Data curation, Visualization, Investigation. Yuki Ide: Data curation, Visualization. Kazuo Umemura: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Asuka Ishizawa: Data curation, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Our work was financially supported by JST SICORP Japan (grant number JPMJSC19E1).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2023.134508.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
